text:
# Online is gaining ground in the drugstore industry

[Stand of Sectors](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/stand-van-sectoren/index.html)

[Retail](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/retail/index.html)

03/10/2025 • 7 minutes reading

[Gerarda Westerhuis](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/authors/gerarda-westerhuis/index.html)

[Ward van der Stee](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/authors/ward-van-der-stee/index.html)

The drugstore industry is growing in volume and turnover. Yet it is not an easy market. For example, online competition is growing and consumers are price-conscious. On the other hand, the population is aging and there is increasing demand for self-care and cosmetic products. Social media, including community building, offer opportunities and challenges.

## Download the complete report

Read more about economic developments in the retail sector in the report 'State of Retail (October 2025)'.

- [Stand of Retail (October 2025)

  859 KB](https://assets.abnamro.com/api/public/content/stand-van-retail-oktober-2025.pdf)

The demand for health and cosmetics products is on the rise. More and more consumers are focusing on health, wellness and prevention, which has led to an increase in sales of vitamins, minerals and self-care products for minor ailments – the so-called self-care market. Consumers are increasingly treating mild health complaints themselves.

At the same time, the demand for cosmetics also continues to rise, especially in segments such as hair care, skin care and premium products. [Consultant agency McKinsey](https://www.mckinsey.com/industries/consumer-packaged-goods/our-insights/a-close-look-at-the-global-beauty-industry-in-2025? cid=mgp_opr-eml-nsl-omcknsl-mgp-glb--&hlkid=3054b0ae41e84a29ba4120977e219f69&hctky=15670902&hdpid=d36ac1af-9ab9-4b69-a589-4b59c851c58f) expects this market to grow by 5 percent annually worldwide until 2030. We also expect this increase for the Dutch market. The growth in cosmetics is influenced, among other things, by trends on social media and platforms such as TikTok and Instagram. There, these products are in the spotlight, often recommended by influencers. As a result, young people have also become increasingly interested in personal care.

## The market for self-care and cosmetics

The drugstore industry in the Netherlands will account for 78 percent of the total self-care market of more than 1 billion euros in turnover in 2024, show data from [Neprofarm](https://neprofarm.nl/zelfzorgmarkt-2024/), the trade association and knowledge and expertise center in the field of self-care. The main products that drugstores sell are cough and cold remedies, painkillers, and vitamins and minerals.

In addition, consumers spent 3.7 billion euros on personal care in 2024. This is evident from the 2024 annual report of the Dutch Cosmetics Association (NCV). Drugstores account for more than a third of the personal care market, according to data for the years 2017-2020 from Statistics Netherlands (CBS). This concerns products such as perfumes, skin care, decorative cosmetics, and hair and oral care.

## Self-employed are losing ground

Within the drugstore industry we have been seeing a decline in the number of self-employed people for some time now. Between 2015 and 2025 there will be a decrease from 324 to 203 branches, according to Locatus data; that is a decrease of more than 37 percent (see Figure 2). Chains have better scale, purchasing advantages, and marketing power and operate as many as 2,317 stores.

Self-employed people have a more difficult time, especially in being able to compete on price. For example, the turnover of independent drugstores in the self-care market has been decreasing annually since 2022, while it is growing significantly at chain drugstores, says [Neprofarm](https://neprofarm.nl/zelfzorgmarkt-2024/).

## Online channel competition has increased significantly

Aggregated and anonymized transaction data from ABN AMRO shows growth in spending on drugstores. It also becomes clear that expenditure via the online channel, both pure online stores and omnichannel players, is growing faster than expenditure in physical stores (see Figure 3).

This is also evident from data from Thuiswinkel.org, an interest group for all (online) stores in the Netherlands. The share of online self-care and personal care has grown from 13 percent in 2019 to 17 percent in 2024, the organization said. In 2024, consumers spent an average of 21 euros per online order. Part of this online spending, namely 11 percent, was spent across the border.

If we zoom in on spending in physical stores, we see clear growth after the corona crisis (see Figure 4). During corona, the growth of the drugstore channel was minimal, because many people bought drugstore products at the supermarket instead of at the drugstore, because they preferred to visit only one store. However, after the corona period, we will see steady growth in spending again. What is also striking are the peaks in December each year, caused by holidays such as Sinterklaas and Christmas.

As mentioned, the transaction data also show the significant growth of the online channel since the corona period. This growth is now leveling off somewhat (see Figure 5). While physical stores show a peak in spending in December, this applies to the online channel in November, the month in which Black Friday falls. The last quarter of the year is therefore important for the turnover of drugstores. Consumers are looking for gifts, which increases the demand for products such as perfumes, care products and gift sets.

In addition, and therefore not included in the transaction data, more and more purchases are made via platforms such as Bol or Amazon, or specialized beauty websites. These platforms benefit from economies of scale and often offer competitive prices and fast delivery. Once a consumer has a favorite product, he can purchase it online at the lowest price. In addition, we are seeing cosmetics and health product brands choosing to sell directly to consumers through their own websites, reducing the role of traditional drugstores as a middleman.

##Dates and disclaimer

ABN AMRO's transaction data is aggregated based on private accounts and completely anonymized. The data says nothing about spending by non-residents and spending on business accounts. We use transaction data to gain insight into economic flows. The results of the research cannot be traced back to a natural person and are exclusively intended for research into economic trends.

## Price-conscious consumer

Consumers are critical, they look for lower prices and save through private labels or promotions. For example, we see that consumers are going to supermarket discounters and buying private labels more often. We also see the latter at drugstores. Although the share of private labels in cosmetics, for example, is lower than in supermarkets, this share has now grown to 24 percent. The promotional pressure has also increased further; in 2024, 43.4 percent of personal care products were sold in the promotion. The promotional pressure was 41.7 percent in 2023. This is evident from the 2024 annual report of the Dutch Cosmetics Association (NCV).

Another way to save money is to buy a so-called dupe, a cheaper alternative to a more expensive branded product. It is very similar to the original in appearance, operation and quality. Dupes are mainly found in cosmetics and care products and in fashion. They are popular because they offer similar features at a lower price. Data from recent research by ABN AMRO shows that 35 percent of young people aged 18-25 currently buy dupes in cosmetics to save money.

[Read also: Balancing between spending and saving](/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/retail/balancing-between-spenderen-en-besparen.html)

The drugstore industry is dominated by chains that cater to price-conscious consumers. They often position themselves as price-conscious providers with a wide range. They charge low prices and regularly make offers, especially on popular products such as shampoo, toothpaste and skin care. They are known for their discount promotions and bundle discounts. This attracts price-conscious consumers and stimulates impulse purchases.

To face this competition from the chains, independent drugstores can focus on customer and store experience, advice and service and local ties. Focusing on a specific segment, such as nutritional supplements, sports nutrition and other health products, can also be rewarding.

## Social media: a curse or a blessing?

Social media offers drugstores great opportunities to increase their visibility, reach a younger audience and capitalize on popular trends. Consumers are increasingly discovering, comparing and purchasing products via social media. These platforms serve as a source of inspiration and direct sales channel. Purchasing decisions are strongly influenced by advertisements, influencers and user content. Visual platforms such as Instagram and TikTok are particularly effective for products that rely heavily on visual presentation, such as cosmetics and care products. Influencers and users share their experiences with these products, which can strengthen credibility and the urge to purchase.

Previous research by ABN AMRO among more than 1,000 consumers conducted by Panelwizard shows that 22.4 percent of respondents have become more aware of nutrition and health through social media; and 10.3 percent have tried supplements.

[Read also: Consumer wants to eat healthy, but encounters price and convenience](/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/retail/consument-want-gezond-eten-but-en-stuit-op-prijs-en-convenience.html)

However, not all information shared via social media is reliable. For example, consumers may purchase products based on incorrect claims or trends that are not scientifically substantiated. This can lead to disappointment or even health risks, which can also damage the image of drugstores. This is one of the reasons why trade organization [Dutch Cosmetics Association](https://www.ncv-cosmetica.nl/) (NCV) launched the website Mijncosmetica.nl in early 2024. “We founded this website as a counterpoint to all the misinformation on social media that misleads consumers,” the site says. “With clear and substantiated information, we ensure that the correct information is available on My Cosmetics and we distinguish the facts from the myths.”

The self-care industry has also considered this dilemma. For example, drugstores have developed the Self-Care Online Quality Mark. A large part of the online self-care market is now affiliated with the [Keurmerk Zelfzorg Online](https://www.careality.nl/2024/11/05/veilig-zelfzorggeneemiddels-kopen-ook-online/) and can be recognized by the Green Plus.

## Building Communities

The well-known chains mainly focus on traditional forms of customer loyalty, such as savings cards and discount promotions. Offering a wide range also makes it attractive for many consumers to come there. For example, Kruitvat offers products such as detergent, toilet paper and even toys.

However, many brands in the cosmetics and self-care industries are increasingly using community building to create a strong emotional bond with their customers and develop a sense of community. By actively involving customers and being transparent, these companies not only increase sales, but also build long-term loyalty. Social media play a crucial role in this, because they enable direct and personal interaction with the target group. Sephora, a major cosmetics and perfume retailer, is a textbook example of a brand that takes community building to the next level. Through a combination of loyalty programs, social media, inclusivity and education, Sephora has built a global network of engaged customers.

## More information

Read more about economic developments in the retail sector in the report 'State of Retail (October 2025)'.

- [Stand of Retail (October 2025)

  859 KB](https://assets.abnamro.com/api/public/content/stand-van-retail-oktober-2025.pdf)

## Read more in the retail sector

Today's store is more than just a pantry. It offers customers advice and inspiration and is the place to be in contact with customers. Due to the Ukraine crisis and significant inflation, consumer confidence has fallen sharply and this affects consumer behavior. Retailers are also faced with significant cost increases in the areas of purchasing, rent, personnel costs, Covid recovery payments and energy. Circumstances remain challenging for many retailers even after Covid.
